title: practical english usage - Part 63
content:
Note the pronunciation: /sə'pəust tə/,       /sə'pəuzd tɔ/.

Bill was supposed to get back home yesterday.

Cats are supposed to be afraid of dogs, but ours isn't.

We   can use be supposed to to say that something is or was normal or expected.

3 be   supposed           to We ought to have finished painting the house by the end of next week.

Ten o'clock. She should have arrived at her office by now. I'll try calling her.

been completed by now or at a future time.

We   can also use this structure to talk about actions which  we  expect to have not (     James should get back home yesterday...) James should have got back home yesterday. Has anybody seen him?

happened.

guesses or draw conclusions about things which  are not certain to have However, we  can use should have / ought to have + past participle to make We do not normally use should + infinitive to talk about the past.

2 past:   should        have      I ought       to   have      . ..

The weather ought to improve after the weekend.

Henry ought to be here soon - he left home at six.

Ought  to (►  76.3) is used in a similar way, but is much less common.

'We're spending the winter in Miami.' 'That should be nice.' I'll phone Ruth - she should be home by now.

I've bought three loaves - that should be enough.

probable now or in the future: it is expected, normal or logical.

Should can be used to express the deduction or conclusion that something is 1  present      or  future probable):     should,       ought       to,   etc 70 deduction       (deciding      that    something        is Section 7 Modal    Auxiliary      Verbs grammar »71     chances: may, might and could but I thought 1 might be ill.

I felt very hot and tired. Perhaps I was ill. (     .. .-Ltnighl be-ilk) (except in indirect speech).

Might/could + infinitive is not normally used to talk about past possibility 7 past:   might/could            + infinitive       not   used 7 may go to Scotland.' 'What?* 7 said I might go to Scotland.' Might/could is used in past indirect speech when may was used in direct speech.

6 indirect      speech:     might/could (= ... perhaps you would feel better.) If you went to bed for an hour, you might feel better.

(= Perhaps you would get hurt.) Don't play with knives. You might/could get hurt.

perhaps).

Might and could (but not may) can have a conditional meaning (= would 5 might       meaning       'would     perhaps' Couldn't is possible with the same meaning as can't, but is less common.

You can't win. (= You certainly won't win.) You might not win. (= Perhaps you won't win.) - You might win. (= Perhaps you will win.) She can't be at home. (= She is certainly not at home.) She may not be at home. (= Perhaps she is not at home.) - She may be at home. (= Perhaps she is at home.) possible that. .. not...) and with can't (= It is not possible that...) Compare: There are two ways to make may/might negative: with may/might not (= It is 4 two    negatives:      may/might           not    and   can't Might you go camping? (less natural than Do you think you may/might...?) Might can be used in direct questions, but this is rather formal.

Do you think you may go camping this summer?

But may is possible in indirect questions (for example after Do you think).

Has Emma  gone running I wonder? (     May Emmo rhave gone-running?) not Are you likely to go camping this summer?  (     May you-go camping...?) May is not normally used to ask about the chance of something happening.

3 questions:      may     not   used Joe might come with me. (perhaps a 30%   chance) / may go to London tomorrow, (perhaps a 50% chance) when people think something is possible but not very likely. Compare: definite or more hesitant than may, suggesting a smaller chance - they are used they are used to talk about the present or future. Might and could are less Might and could are not often used as past forms of may and can: more often 2 may,     might       and   could:     the   difference not Emma  can be out running not II can rain-thl^after noon.
